Title: Systematic analysis of the non-specific lipid transfer protein gene family in Nicotiana tabacum reveal its potential roles in stress responses

Plant_Genes.Notes_or_Other_Important_Info: NtLTPI.38 (also referred to as NtLTP1.38 in some parts of the text) belongs to type I nsLTPs. Overexpression did not cause obvious growth penalties; instead, it increased plant height and stem circumference under field conditions, suggesting pleiotropic effects on both growth and stress responses.
Plant_Genes.Summary_key_Findings_of_Core_Gene:
1. NtLTPI.38 is a type I non-specific lipid transfer protein gene in Nicotiana tabacum whose expression is strongly induced by drought and other abiotic stresses and shows high expression in stems and leaves.
2. Transgenic overexpression of NtLTPI.38 in tobacco (cv. K326) increases plant height and stem circumference under field conditions.
3. Under mannitol-simulated drought stress, NtLTPI.38 overexpression lines exhibit enhanced drought tolerance, with maintained growth, increased lateral root number, and reduced wilting compared with wild type.
4. NtLTPI.38 overexpression reduces accumulation of O2- and H2O2 and lowers MDA content under drought, indicating enhanced antioxidant defense as a mechanism for improved drought tolerance.
Plant_Genes.Omics_Data: RNA-seq datasets of Nicotiana tabacum cv. Honghuadajinyuan under drought, black shank infection and Ralstonia solanacearum infection (NCBI SRA BioProjects PRJNA576540, PRJNA320659, PRJNA451198) were used to profile NtLTPI.38 expression.
Plant_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Transgenic overexpression of NtLTPI.38 in tobacco increases drought tolerance by enhancing antioxidant defense (lower O2- and H2O2 accumulation) and increasing lateral root number, leading to reduced wilting under mannitol-simulated drought.
Plant_Genes.Regulatory_Mechanism: Overexpression of NtLTPI.38 enhances antioxidant defense under drought, as evidenced by reduced accumulation of superoxide (O2-) and hydrogen peroxide (H2O2), lower MDA content, and increased lateral root formation, thereby mitigating drought-induced oxidative damage.
Plant_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: Non-specific lipid transfer protein (nsLTP), type I; contains the conserved eight-cysteine motif (ECM) and two conserved pentapeptide domains T/S-X-X-D-R/K and P-Y-X-I-S characteristic of type I LTPs.
Plant_Genes.Expression_Pattern: NtLTPI.38 is strongly induced by drought in RNA-seq and qRT-PCR analyses; its expression is also induced by salt and high-temperature stresses but shows little response to low temperature; it is induced by IAA and slightly by GA and is inhibited by MeJA; tissue profiling shows high expression in stems, leaves and flowers but low expression in roots.
Plant_Genes.Key_Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Mannitol-induced drought stress (100 and 200 mM D-mannitol) applied to seedlings on MS medium and to 50-day-old seedlings in a floating water culture system; comparison with normally watered controls under the same growth conditions.
Plant_Genes.Research_Topic: Abiotic stress tolerance (drought), antioxidant defense, root development and plant growth regulation in tobacco.
Plant_Genes.Trait_Category: Abiotic stress tolerance (drought stress tolerance) and root system development in a crop plant.
Plant_Genes.Other_Phenotypic_Effects: Under normal conditions, NtLTPI.38 overexpression plants showed increased plant height and stem circumference in the field and slightly longer primary roots compared with wild type; under 200 mM mannitol treatment, overexpression lines developed significantly more lateral roots than wild type.
Plant_Genes.Experimental_Materials: Nicotiana tabacum L. cv. Honghuadajinyuan seedlings for RNA-seq-based expression analysis under drought, black shank and Ralstonia solanacearum infection; N. tabacum cv. Honghuadajinyuan seedlings for hormone and abiotic stress qRT-PCR assays; transgenic N. tabacum cv. K326 NtLTPI.38 overexpression lines (OE3, OE17) and wild-type K326 plants for functional characterization in field and controlled drought (mannitol) conditions.
Plant_Genes.Experimental_Methods: Genome-wide identification of nsLTP genes using HMMER against tobacco genomes; RNA-seq analysis (TPM, DESeq2) under drought, black shank and Ralstonia solanacearum infection; qRT-PCR for expression under hormone (ABA, SA, IAA, MeJA, GA) and abiotic stresses (drought, salt, cold, heat) and in different tissues; construction of PART-CAM-NtLTPI.38 overexpression vector and Agrobacterium-mediated transformation; PCR confirmation of transgenic lines; field growth observation and measurements of plant height and stem circumference; mannitol (100 and 200 mM) treatments on seedlings in MS medium and floating water culture; measurement of primary root length and lateral root number; NBT staining for O2- accumulation; assays of MDA, O2- and H2O2 contents using enzyme activity kits.
Plant_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Stable transgenic overexpression in tobacco (Agrobacterium-mediated transformation), followed by phenotypic analysis under mannitol-induced drought stress, NBT staining for O2- accumulation, and biochemical measurements of MDA, O2- and H2O2 contents.
Plant_Genes.Field_Trials: NtLTPI.38 overexpression lines (OE3, OE17) and wild-type K326 were grown in the field; overexpression increased plant height and stem circumference under normal field conditions.
Plant_Genes.Gene_Discovery_or_Cloning_Method: NtLTPI.38 was identified as a member of the tobacco nsLTP gene family through HMMER-based genome-wide searches and family classification, then selected as a drought-inducible candidate based on RNA-seq and qRT-PCR expression profiles; its coding sequence was amplified and cloned into the PART-CAM overexpression vector for functional analysis.
Plant_Genes.Breeding_Application_Value: Overexpression of NtLTPI.38 enhances drought tolerance and promotes growth in tobacco by improving antioxidant capacity and lateral root formation, indicating its potential utility as a candidate gene for breeding tobacco cultivars with improved drought resistance and vigor under water-limited conditions.
